content.full_text:
F AIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Sireet • Sacramento. CA 95812-0807 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Michael H. Miller 
City Attorney 
City of Arcadia 
240 W. Huntington Drive 
Post Office Box 60021 
Arcadia, California 91066-6021 
Re: Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-99-019 
Dear Mr. Miller: 
February 24, 1999 
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of council member Gino 
Roncelli regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' 
QUESTION 
Is council member Roncelli disqualified from participating in decisions about the 
development of certain land in Santa Anita Park, because he owns horses that race at Santa Anita 
and he has received over $250 in income from the Los Angeles Turf Club. Or is the Los Angeles 
Turf Club not considered a source of income to council member Roncelli, because it merely acts 
as a conduit for money from bettors? 
CONCLUSION 
If council member Roncelli has received $250 or more in income from the Los Angeles 
Turf Club in the past 12 months, the Turf Club is a source of income to him. Council member 
Roncelli has a conflict of interest and must refrain from participating in decisions about the 
development of portions of Santa Anita Park if it is substantially likely that the decisions would 
have a material financial effect on the Los Angeles Turf Club or on any business entity that is a 
, Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations. 


File No. A-99-019 
Page 2 
parent, subsidiary, or "otherwise related" to the Turf Club (as defined in regulation 18703.I(c) 
and (d)). 
FACTS 
Mr. Gino Roncelli was elected to the Arcadia City Council in April of 1998. The five 
Arcadia councilmembers are elected at large to represent the entire city rather than districts. 
Arcadia is the home of the Santa Anita Park, a horse racing site and adjacent parking lot 
area. The total site which comprises approximately 317 acres is owned by the Magna 
International Corporation ("Magna"). It is anticipated that the development of the property will 
be sought by Magna Development. Magna International is the parent corporation, which has 
numerous subsidiaries, including Magna Development. Continuing down the corporate 
structure, the Santa Anita Companies are a subsidiary of Magna Development and the Los 
Angeles Turf Club is a subsidiary corporation of the Santa Anita Companies.2 The Los Angeles 
Turf Club is the operator of the racetrack and functions as the racing association as described 
below. The Turf Club does not function in the arena ofland development. 
Magna is contemplating development of part of its parking lot area into various 
commercial uses. This will entail the processing of a development application before the city 
which will include rezoning and related discretionary legislative type decisions by the city 
council after review by the city planuing commission. This will also involve environmental 
review by the city council. The financial effect of these decisions on Magna is material. These 
decisions do not involve the racetrack (Turf Club) or horse racing. They involve separate uses 
that will most likely include theaters, restaurants, commercial and residential. Parking for horse 
racing will still be in accordance with city code and parking requirements. 
Prior to his election as a city councilmember, Mr. Roncelli owned race horses which, on 
occasion, participated in the racing program at Santa Anita. His horses also race at other race 
track sites in California. He currently has an ownership interest in some horses that may, within 
twelve months of any city decisions, derive revenue from racing at the Santa Anita Park in 
excess of $250. 
Santa Anita Park is a state licensed site for race horse owners to enter their horses into 
competition. The Turf Club has no direct relationship with the horse owners, including Mr. 
Roncelli. They operate the horse racing venue subject to complete regulation by the State Horse 
Racing Board. You have provided the following description of horse racing in California. 
2 The facts presented in this letter were those provided in your incoming request for advice, as 
supplemented by our telephone conversations on February 17 and 18th. 


File No. A-99-0l9 
Page 3 
Horse Racing. The applicable statutory law for horse racing is set forth in the State 
Business and Professions Code (Horse Racing Law) commencing at section 19400. Rules and 
regulations regarding horse racing are contained at title 4 of division 4 of the California Code of 
Regulations. 
Pursuant to the above legal authority, under the system known as pari-mutual wagering, 
the racing association acts as a stakeholder for all wagers, deducting from each pari-mutual 
wagering pool the statutory "takeout," which includes the state license fees, the percentage 
deduction for purses, and the race track's revenue percentage. The racing association for the 
Santa Anita Park is the Los Angeles Turf Club. 
"Purses" constitute what is paid to the horse owner. The purses for race meetings are 
determined by established rate schedules negotiated in advance of a racing meet by the 
Thoroughbred Owners of California ("TOC") and the Turf Club. Pursuant to state law, race 
tracks in California are accorded a slot of the calendar to conduct competition at their location. 
The Turf Club operates Santa Anita Park for this purpose during the December - April time 
period. This time slot is also referred to as the racing meet. 
The actual purses to be paid for anyone race, or for the day's races, initially are 
determined by the racing secretary, an employee of the Turf Club, based on projections of the 
handle (amount wagered). The purse is often revised via negotiations between the TOC and the 
Turf Club during the course of the race meeting based on the actual handle. Approximately 
11,000 horse owners, including Mr. Roncelli, are subject to the what is negotiated by the TOC 
and the Turf Club. He is not a director or officer of the Turf Club. Whether he is an official 
member or not, as a horse owner, he is in effect represented by the Turf Club on the matters 
referred to in this paragraph. 
The "handle" which is the amount bet (wagered) comes from both on track betting and 
off-track betting. On track betting makes up less than 26 percent of wagers placed. The 
remainder comes from off-track betting (simulcast wagering) both in and outside of the state. 
The amount of the total purse is determined through negotiations between the TOC and 
the Turf Club prior to the race meet. Usually, revenue is paid to those owners whose horses 
finish one through six in any given race. This revenue is a percentage established by statute. Of 
course, the races are competitive and the results are a matter of speculation. 
In sum, a race horse owner may derive revenue ifhis or her horse finishes within a 
required place in a race. If the owner is entitled to revenues, their source is the "handle" which is 
the money bet by members ofthe public who attend the racing event at Santa Anita Park and at 
off-track betting facilities. The amount of the purse and its percentage allocation is pre-
determined before the racing meet based on the prospective "handle." Similarly, the "handle" is 
the source of income to the Turf Club. 


File No. A-99-019 
Page 4 
Horses are selected for a racing meet and for participation in specific races only if they 
qualifY as determined by the racing secretary based solely on objective criteria including the sex, 
age, past performance and racing times of the horses. 
You question whether the Turf Club is a source of income to council member Roncelli 
that would create a conflict of interest with respect to decisions about development of Santa 
Anita Park, or whether the Turf Club is not a source of income because it acts as a conduit for 
money from bettors. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their 
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the 
financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. 
To say that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within 
the meaning of the Act, is to conclude that it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental 
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's economic interests. 
(Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) 
1. The conflict-or-interest rules apply to public officials who are making, 
participating in making, and influencing governmental decisions. 
As a member of the Arcadia City Council, Mr. Roncelli is a public official subject to the 
Act's conflict-of-interest restrictions. (Section 82048; Regulation 18701.) The Act's conflict-of-
interest provisions apply to an official when the official makes, participates in making, or in any 
way attempts to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official knows or has reason to know he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) The 
Commission's regulations describe in some detail what constitutes making, participating in 
making, and influencing a governmental decision. (Regulations 18702.1, 18702.2, and 18702.3, 
respectively.) There are also important exceptions to these rules. (Regulation 18702.4.) 
In this instance, you ask whether council member Roncelli can participate in various 
upcoming city council decisions about the development of Santa Anita Park. Magna's 
contemplated development of part of Santa Anita Park into theaters, restaurants, and other 
commercial and residential uses, will entail the processing of a development application before 
the city. The development application will include rezoning and related discretionary legislative 
type decisions by the city council after review by the city planning commission. An 
environmental review by the city council will also be involved. 


2. Identifying economic interests. 
File No. A-99-0l9 
Page 5 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from economic 
interests. The "economic interests" from which conflicts of interest may arise are defined in 
Regulations 18703-18703.5. There are six kinds of such economic interests: 
• 
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a 
direct or indirect investmene of$l,OOO or more. (Regulation 18703.1(a).} 
• 
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she is a 
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position ofmanagement. 
(Regulation 18703.1 (b).) 
• 
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct 
or indirect interest of$l,OOO or more. (Regulation 18703.2.) 
• 
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income which aggregates to 
$250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Regulation 18703.3.) 
• 
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts 
aggregate to $300 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Regulation 18703.4.) 
• 
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets, 
or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family -
this is known as the 
"personal financial effects" rule. (Regulation 18703.5.) 
You ask whether the Los Angeles Turf Club is a source of income to council member 
Roncelli. A public official has an economic interest in sources of income totaling $250 or more, 
if that sum was received by the official within 12 months preceding a governmental decision. 
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.} Under the Act, when a public official receives income 
from a particular source -
including an individual, a business entity, or a nonprofit organization 
-
that individual or entity is considered a source of income to the official. 
In this case, council member Roncelli may have received over $250 from the Los 
Angeles Turf Club in the past. He also has an ownership interest in some horses that may, within 
12 months of upcoming city council decisions, derive revenue from racing at Santa Anita Park in 
3 An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent 
child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the 
official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent 
interest or greater. (Section 87103.) 


File No. A-99-0l9 
Page 6 
excess of $250. You agree that section 82030 defines income broadly as a payment received, 
and that when Mr. Roncelli receives prize money from a racing meet, those funds can accurately 
be characterized as income. However, you question whether the source of income is the Turf 
Club or the individual bettors. 
Under the Act, the corporation -
the Los Angeles Turf Club -
is a distinct legal entity 
that is considered a source of income to Mr. Roncelli. Despite the fact that the funds the Turf 
Club distributes come from the money bet by members of the public who attend the racing event 
at Santa Anita Park and at off-track betting facilities, the Turf Club is the corporate entity that is 
the source of income to Mr. Roncelli. You stated that the Los Angeles Turf Club is charged with 
administering the accounts, so the Turf Club issues the checks to race horse owners such as Mr. 
Roncelli. In addition, the amount of the "purse" is determined through negotiations between the 
Turf Club and the TOC. These entities establish the rate schedule setting how much is paid to 
horse owners. 
Under the Act, we do not "look through" the corporation to find that the thousands of 
individuals who place bets on the races at Santa Anita are sources of income to the council 
member in this situation, rather than the Los Angeles Turf Club. For example, in the Zweben 
Advice Letter, No. 1-94-380, we advised that a public official's winnings from betting at horse 
races constituted income from the corporation that operated the racetrack under the Act. 
General1y, the Act considers clients, customers, or other sources of income to a business 
to be sources of income to a public official when the official owns a ten percent or greater 
interest in the business. If the official owns ten percent or more of a business, the pro rata share 
of any income to that business entity is a source of income to the official. (Section 82030.) 
In addition, the Commission has in rare circumstances "pierced" through entities, such as 
corporations, which are the apparent source of income, to treat the control1ing person as an 
additional source of income or economic interest of the official's. (See e.g., Lahr Advice Letter, 
No. 1-98-298; Brown Advice Letter, A-97-l22; Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-80-03-069; In re 
Nord (\ 983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6). In this situation, however, the thousands of individuals who bet on 
the horse races do not have any ownership or controlling interest in the Los Angeles Turf Club. 
They do not direct or control the income to council member Roncelli. They are not the "true" or 
"additional" source of income to council member Roncelli whom we should look through the 
Los Angeles Turf Club corporation to reach.4 
4 The facts of the Ko/key Advice Letter, No. A-95-014, are distinguishable from these, as in that case the 
official received payments from two parties to a dispute (a foreign and a domestic airline company) for providing 
arbitration services, and the payments were routed through the American Arbitration Association. There, we 
advised that the official did not have an economic interest in the American Arbitration Association which acted as 
an intermediary. The two airlines would be economic interests of the official. 


File No. A-99-019 
Page 7 
Here, the payments that council member Roncelli receives ifhis race horse wins at Santa 
Anita are from the Los Angeles Turf Club, and the amount of the total purse paid to horse 
owners is determined through negotiations between the Turf Club and the TOC prior to the race. 
The Los Angeles Turf Club is considered a source of income to the council member in this 
situation. 
a. Parents and subsidiaries. Because multiple business entities can be closely 
affiliated, Commission regulations provide that if an official has an economic interest in a 
business entity, he or she also has an economic interest in any business entity that is a parent or 
subsidiary of or is "otherwise related" to that entity. (Regulation l8703.l(c) and (d).) A parent-
subsidiary relationship exists among corporations, when one corporation directly or indirectly 
owns shares possessing more than 50 percent of the voting power of another corporation. 
Regulation 18703.1 states as follows regarding related business entities: 
"(c) Parent, Subsidiary, Otherwise Related Business Entity. An 
official has a fmancial interest in a decision within the meaning of 
Government Code section 87100 ifit is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally, on a business entity which 
is a parent or subsidiary of, or is otherwise related to, a business 
entity in which the official has one of the interests defined in 
Government Code section 87103(a), (c) or (d). 
(d) Parent, Subsidiary, Otherwise Related Business Entity, 
defined. 
(1) Parent-subsidiary. A parent-subsidiary relationship exists 
when one corporation directly or indirectly owns shares possessing 
more than 50 percent of the voting power of another corporation. 
(2) Otherwise related business entity. Business entities, 
including corporations, partuerships, joint ventures and any other 
organizations and enterprises operated for profit, which do not 
have a parent-subsidiary relationship are otherwise related if any 
one of the following three tests is met: 
(A) One business entity has a controlling ownership interest in 
the other business entity. 
(B) There is shared management and control between the 
entities. In determining whether there is shared management and 
control, consideration should be given to the following factors: 
(i) The same person or substantially the same person owns and 
manages the two entities; 
(ii) There are common or commingled funds or assets; 


File No. A-99-0l9 
Page 8 
(iii) The business entities share the use of the same offices or 
employees, or otherwise share activities, resources or personnel on 
a regular basis; 
(iv) There is otherwise a regular and close working relationship 
between the entities; or 
(C) A controlling owner (50% or greater interest as a 
shareholder or as a general partner) in one entity also is a 
controlling owner in the other entity." 
Under regulation I 8703.I(c)-(d), because council member Roncelli has a financial 
interest in the Los Angeles Turf Club, he also has a financial interest in its parent corporation and 
in any otherwise related corporations. Assuming the entities in the corporate hierarchy, from 
Magna International Corporation down to Magna Development, the Santa Anita Companies, and 
the Los Angeles Turf Club, are subsidiaries or otherwise related business entities under 
regulation 18703.1(d), then Mr. Roncelli must disqualify himself from participating in any 
decision of the Arcadia City Council that will have a material financial effect on these entities. 
3. Deciding which materiality standards apply, and using those standards to decide 
if there will be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect. 
The facts you have provided state that Magna will likely file a development application 
with the city and that its property will be the subject of zoning, rezoning and other decisions. In 
this case Magna is considered to be directly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18704.l(a).) If 
an official has an interest in a business entity that is directly involved in a decision, the financial 
effect is deemed material. (Regulation 18705.3.) Because council member Roncelli has an 
economic interest in Magna, he must refrain from participating in decisions about the 
development of Santa Anita Park. 
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at 
(916) 322-5660. 
SGC:HPW:tls 
]oI991ttrsI99019.wpd 
Sincerely, 
Steven G. Churchwell 
General Counsel 
~P.LOL~{1y 
By: 
Hyla P. Wagner 
Staff Counsel, Legal Division 
CORRECTED LETTER: JUNE 7, 1999

sections.question:
Is council member Roncelli disqualified from participating in decisions about the 
development of certain land in Santa Anita Park, because he owns horses that race at Santa Anita 
and he has received over $250 in income from the Los Angeles Turf Club. Or is the Los Angeles 
Turf Club not considered a source of income to council member Roncelli, because it merely acts 
as a conduit for money from bettors?
sections.conclusion:
If council member Roncelli has received $250 or more in income from the Los Angeles 
Turf Club in the past 12 months, the Turf Club is a source of income to him. Council member 
Roncelli has a conflict of interest and must refrain from participating in decisions about the 
development of portions of Santa Anita Park if it is substantially likely that the decisions would 
have a material financial effect on the Los Angeles Turf Club or on any business entity that is a 
,Page 2 
parent, subsidiary, or "otherwise related" to the Turf Club (as defined in regulation 18703.I(c) 
and (d)).

the source of income to Mr. Roncelli. You stated that the Los Angeles Turf Club is charged with 
administering the accounts, so the Turf Club issues the checks to race horse owners such as Mr. 
Roncelli. In addition, the amount of the "purse" is determined through negotiations between the 
Turf Club and the TOC. These entities establish the rate schedule setting how much is paid to 
horse owners. 
Under the Act, we do not "look through" the corporation to find that the thousands of 
individuals who place bets on the races at Santa Anita are sources of income to the council 
member in this situation, rather than the Los Angeles Turf Club. For example, in the Zweben 
Advice Letter, No. 1-94-380, we advised that a public official's winnings from betting at horse 
races constituted income from the corporation that operated the racetrack under the Act. 
General1y, the Act considers clients, customers, or other sources of income to a business 
to be sources of income to a public official when the official owns a ten percent or greater 
interest in the business. If the official owns ten percent or more of a business, the pro rata share 
of any income to that business entity is a source of income to the official. (Section 82030.) 
In addition, the Commission has in rare circumstances "pierced" through entities, such as 
corporations, which are the apparent source of income, to treat the control1ing person as an 
additional source of income or economic interest of the official's. (See e.g., Lahr Advice Letter, 
No. 1-98-298; Brown Advice Letter, A-97-l22; Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-80-03-069; In re 
Nord (\ 983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6). In this situation, however, the thousands of individuals who bet on 
the horse races do not have any ownership or controlling interest in the Los Angeles Turf Club. 
They do not direct or control the income to council member Roncelli. They are not the "true" or 
"additional" source of income to council member Roncelli whom we should look through the 
Los Angeles Turf Club corporation to reach.4 
4 The facts of the Ko/key Advice Letter, No. A-95-014, are distinguishable from these, as in that case the 
official received payments from two parties to a dispute (a foreign and a domestic airline company) for providing 
arbitration services, and the payments were routed through the American Arbitration Association. There, we 
advised that the official did not have an economic interest in the American Arbitration Association which acted as 
an intermediary. The two airlines would be economic interests of the official.  
Here, the payments that council member Roncelli receives ifhis race horse wins at Santa 
Anita are from the Los Angeles Turf Club, and the amount of the total purse paid to horse 
owners is determined through negotiations between the Turf Club and the TOC prior to the race. 
The Los Angeles Turf Club is considered a source of income to the council member in this 
situation. 
a. Parents and subsidiaries. Because multiple business entities can be closely 
affiliated, Commission regulations provide that if an official has an economic interest in a 
business entity, he or she also has an economic interest in any business entity that is a parent or 
subsidiary of or is "otherwise related" to that entity. (Regulation l8703.l(c) and (d).) A parent-
subsidiary relationship exists among corporations, when one corporation directly or indirectly 
owns shares possessing more than 50 percent of the voting power of another corporation. 
Regulation 18703.1 states as follows regarding related business entities: 
"(c) Parent, Subsidiary, Otherwise Related Business Entity. An 
official has a fmancial interest in a decision within the meaning of 
Government Code section 87100 ifit is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally, on a business entity which 
is a parent or subsidiary of, or is otherwise related to, a business 
entity in which the official has one of the interests defined in 
Government Code section 87103(a), (c) or (d). 
(d) Parent, Subsidiary, Otherwise Related Business Entity, 
defined. 
(1) Parent-subsidiary. A parent-subsidiary relationship exists 
when one corporation directly or indirectly owns shares possessing 
more than 50 percent of the voting power of another corporation. 
(2) Otherwise related business entity. Business entities, 
including corporations, partuerships, joint ventures and any other 
organizations and enterprises operated for profit, which do not 
have a parent-subsidiary relationship are otherwise related if any 
one of the following three tests is met: 
(A) One business entity has a controlling ownership interest in 
the other business entity. 
(B) There is shared management and control between the 
entities. In determining whether there is shared management and 
control, consideration should be given to the following factors: 
(i) The same person or substantially the same person owns and 
manages the two entities; 
(ii) There are common or commingled funds or assets; 


File No. A-99-0l9  
(iii) The business entities share the use of the same offices or 
employees, or otherwise share activities, resources or personnel on 
a regular basis; 
(iv) There is otherwise a regular and close working relationship 
between the entities; or 
(C) A controlling owner (50% or greater interest as a 
shareholder or as a general partner) in one entity also is a 
controlling owner in the other entity." 
Under regulation I 8703.I(c)-(d), because council member Roncelli has a financial 
interest in the Los Angeles Turf Club, he also has a financial interest in its parent corporation and 
in any otherwise related corporations. Assuming the entities in the corporate hierarchy, from 
Magna International Corporation down to Magna Development, the Santa Anita Companies, and 
the Los Angeles Turf Club, are subsidiaries or otherwise related business entities under 
regulation 18703.1(d), then Mr. Roncelli must disqualify himself from participating in any 
decision of the Arcadia City Council that will have a material financial effect on these entities. 
3. Deciding which materiality standards apply, and using those standards to decide 
if there will be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect. 
The facts you have provided state that Magna will likely file a development application 
with the city and that its property will be the subject of zoning, rezoning and other decisions. In 
this case Magna is considered to be directly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18704.l(a).) If 
an official has an interest in a business entity that is directly involved in a decision, the financial 
effect is deemed material. (Regulation 18705.3.) Because council member Roncelli has an 
economic interest in Magna, he must refrain from participating in decisions about the 
development of Santa Anita Park.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is council member Roncelli disqualified from participating in decisions about the 
development of certain land in Santa Anita Park, because he owns horses that race at Santa Anita 
and he has received over $250 in income from the Los Angeles Turf Club. Or is the Los Angeles 
Turf Club not considered a source of income to council member Roncelli, because it merely acts 
as a conduit for money from bettors?

CONCLUSION: If council member Roncelli has received $250 or more in income from the Los Angeles 
Turf Club in the past 12 months, the Turf Club is a source of income to him. Council member 
Roncelli has a conflict of interest and must refrain from participating in decisions about the 
development of portions of Santa Anita Park if it is substantially likely that the decisions would 
have a material financial effect on the Los Angeles Turf Club or on any business entity that is a 
,Page 2 
parent, subsidiary, or "otherwise related" to the Turf Club (as defined in regulation 18703.I(c) 
and (d)).

Miller City Attorney City of Arcadia 240 W. Huntington Drive Post Office Box 60021 Arcadia, California 91066-6021 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-99-019 Dear Mr. Miller: February 24, 1999 This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of council member Gino Roncelli regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' QUESTION Is council member Roncelli disqualified from participating in decisions about the development of certain land in Santa Anita Park, because he owns horses that race at Santa Anita and he has received over $250 in income from the Los Angeles Turf Club. Or is the Los Angeles Turf Club not considered a source of income to council member Roncelli, because it merely acts as a conduit for money from bettors? CONCLUSION If council member Roncelli has received $250 or more in income from the Los Angeles Turf Club in the past 12 months, the Turf Club is a source of income to him. Council member Roncelli has a conflict of interest and must refrain from participating in decisions about the development of portions of Santa Anita Park if it is substantially likely that the decisions would have a material financial effect on the Los Angeles Turf Club or on any business entity that is a , Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-99-019 Page 2 parent, subsidiary, or "otherwise related" to the Turf Club (as defined in regulation 18703.I(c) and (d)). FACTS Mr. Gino Roncelli was elected to the Arcadia City Council in April of 1998. The five Arcadia councilmembers are elected at large to represent the entire city rather than districts. Arcadia is the home of the Santa Anita Park, a horse racing site and adjacent parking lot area. The total site which comprises approximately 317 acres is owned by the Magna International Corporation ("Magna"). It is anticipated that the development of the property will be sought by Magna Development. Magna International is the parent corporation, which has numerous subsidiaries, including Magna Development. Continuing down the corporate structure, the Santa Anita Companies are a subsidiary of Magna Development and the Los Angeles Turf Club is a subsidiary corporation of the Santa Anita Companies.2 The Los Angeles Turf Club is the operator of the racetrack and functions as the racing association as described below. The Turf Club does not function in the arena ofland development. Magna is contemplating development of part of its parking lot area into various commercial uses. This will entail the processing of a development application before the city which will include rezoning and related discretionary legislative type decisions by the city council after review by the city planuing commission. This will also involve environmental review by the city council. The financial effect of these
